title: Google updates Android Bench with new LLMs, but Gemini still lags behind
gist: Google's updated Android Bench coding benchmark shows its own Gemini 3.1 Pro slipping to fifth place behind Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 (84.5% accuracy leader), GPT 5.4, and Claude Sonnet 5, exposing a competitive gap in Google's core models even as it pushes agentic development and reportedly pays developers for app source code to train on.

insights.claim: On Google's updated Android Bench, Claude Fable 5 leads with a sizeable margin at 84.5 percent accuracy, while Google's own Gemini 3.1 Pro fell to fifth place behind GPT 5.4, Claude Sonnet 5, and Claude Fable 5.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Anthropic and OpenAI outranking Gemini on Google's own Android coding benchmark is a competitive-positioning signal for enterprise coding-tool selection.
insights.quote: Fable 5 lives up to the hype with a sizeable lead at 84.5 percent accuracy in the test.
insights.locator: Even the initial release of Android Bench

insights.claim: Google added eight new models to Android Bench: Claude Fable 5, Claude Sonnet 5, Claude Opus 4.8, GLM 5.2, Kimi K2.7 Code, MiniMax M3, Qwen 3.7 Plus, and Qwen 3.7 Max.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The expanded field, including Chinese open-weight models, maps the competitive set investors should track in AI coding.

insights.claim: Top performers Claude Fable 5 and GPT 5.5 have extremely high operating costs, consuming more than $130 in tokens for the 100-problem, 10-run benchmark, while Gemini 3.1 Pro scored lower but cost only $87 to run.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: The accuracy-vs-cost tradeoff means Gemini competes on price even when it lags on quality, relevant to per-token economics of coding workloads.

insights.claim: Gemini 3.5 Flash, supposedly the cheaper model, had the highest cost on the leaderboard at $165 per run with a 28-hour runtime because it took so much longer to complete the benchmark.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Per-token cheapness can invert into higher total cost when a model is slow, a caution for agentic-workflow cost modeling.
insights.quote: Gemini 3.5 Flash, which is supposed to be cheaper to run than other models, has the highest cost on the leaderboard because it took so much longer to complete the benchmark: $165 per run and a 28-hour runtime.

insights.claim: Gemini's Android coding-performance gap is a problem as Google shifts many projects toward agentic development and would prefer Android developers use its own tools.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Google's weaker coding models undercut its ambition to own the Android agentic-dev workflow, a strategic vulnerability versus Anthropic/OpenAI.
insights.quote: The Android coding performance gap for Google's models is a problem as the company shifts many of its projects toward agentic development.

insights.claim: Google has reportedly been offering to buy application source code from developers for AI training.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Paying for proprietary code as training data signals the escalating cost and scarcity of high-quality coding data to close model gaps.
insights.quote: which may be why Google has reportedly been offering to buy application source code from developers for AI training.

insights.claim: Google switched Android Bench to the Harbor testing framework and re-ran all prior tests to set a new baseline, shifting previously reported scores even though the underlying 100-task test hasn't changed yet.
insights.speaker: Ryan Whitwam
insights.so_what: Framework-driven score shifts mean cross-time benchmark comparisons require caution when evaluating model progress claims.
